schema_version: v0 — component-charter shape; migrates into the intention envelope when MAP.md Q6 lands
what: THE SYSTEM DEVICE — the rack-mount device that owns the host's SYSTEM predicates and serves them device-independently. It owns host-resource data (CPU, memory, disk) and ADVERTISES the callbacks it can serve against them ('I accept a cpu_threshold callback, takes a value'). A caller SUBSCRIBES ('I'll take one, value 80, here's my callback address'); the system device RESOLVES ITS OWN METHOD internally (the caller never learns object.object.method), evaluates the predicate LOCALLY (Law 6 — the trigger reads the host metric INSIDE the device; the caller never sees the raw number), and its SHIM pokes the subscriber on the heartbeat pulse when the line is crossed. `SystemRackmountDevice` subclasses `BaseDevice` (CP1-CP6, Form v0 #2, on which the callback menu is advertised); `SystemRackmountShim` subclasses `BaseShim` and fires the subscriptions on the beat. It is NOT a central scheduler — scheduling is the universal heartbeat + shim + callback mechanism.
why: So no device is coupled to the host, and no host metric is exported just to be tested (Law 6): any device can get a trigger on a host resource, but the reading STAYS HOME — only the wake-up (the poke) crosses the bus. The OS-specific backing of a host reading (load average vs a richer sampler; and, in the same family, privilege via sudo_relay) is swapped BEHIND the advertised callbacks without changing any device that depends on them. This is 'abstract host services device-independently' made concrete — and it corrects the goof: the system rackmount is NOT the rack (the rack is the chassis: bus + shims), and it is NOT a central scheduler (the interval/date/quantity/state enum + the SchedulerService were deleted). It is simply the OWNER of one kind of data other devices want triggers on.
invoke: Construct: `dev = SystemRackmountDevice(sampler=None)` (inject a sampler in proofs). Inspect offerings: `dev.advertises()`. Subscribe: `dev.subscribe('cpu_threshold', address='ops/personal', why=..., value=80)` → a subscription id. Wire it to the beat: `shim = SystemRackmountShim(dev, bus); ground_loop.subscribe(shim)` — the shim pokes subscribers whose line is crossed on each `beat`. Prove: `python3 cairn/system_rackmount/proofs/test_system_rackmount.py` (exit 0 = green).
node_class: device — a rack-mount device on the spine (the spine step after ground_loop). It is the owner of the host's resource predicates; it serves threshold callbacks on them through the universal heartbeat + shim + bus mechanism.
entry: FRESH design to Form v0. The METAPHOR is quarried from UU (a rack holds rack-mount devices; a bus connects them; system services are abstracted so devices are portable), but the shape is Cairn's: a BaseDevice that OWNS host-resource data and ADVERTISES callbacks on it, resolving its own method so a caller stays ignorant of internals, and poking through the shared heartbeat + bus rather than a bespoke scheduler. What is NEW and Cairn's: advertise → subscribe → poke as the registration protocol; Law-6-local-evaluation (the host metric never leaves the device); the system device as a data-OWNER, not a service-registry or scheduler.
state: IMPLEMENTED to the system-device shape and PROVEN (green bare and under the tester). It advertises the cpu_threshold callback; a caller subscribes by menu name + value + address and an unadvertised name is refused loudly (CP1); the device resolves its own predicate internally and evaluates it LOCALLY; end to end through the heartbeat, a beat with the host over the line pokes the subscriber's feed on the real bus exactly once and a beat under the line pokes no one; the poke body carries the caller's own line (80) but the raw reading (95) crosses the bus NOWHERE (Law 6, asserted); the central-scheduler goof is gone (no service()/scheduler API, no trigger-kind enum); it is a device and its shim is a shim (CP1-CP6, Form v0 #2). SUPERSEDED AND DELETED: the shipped SchedulerService (cairn 5cb593a) — a central selector loop reaching out to run other devices, with an interval/date/quantity/state trigger enum — was the goof; both the service and the enum are removed, and `run_driver`/the method-registry it fired onto were retired with the ground_loop strip-down. OPEN EDGES, filed not faked: (a) only cpu_threshold is advertised (via a dependency-free load-average sampler); memory/disk predicates are the SAME shape and mount when a consumer asks; (b) subscriptions are LEVEL-triggered (poke while the predicate holds) — edge-triggering and unsubscribe wait on a real need; (c) advertise/subscribe are direct calls today — expressing them as bus messages (like the poke already is) is a filed edge; (d) the real host sampler (rich per-core CPU, memory, disk) is the OS-specific backing this device will own — a thin edge; the predicate physics is proven by injection; (e) sudo_relay (privilege) is a host service in this same family, built standalone first — it migrates behind this abstraction when a second consumer makes the seam pay.
gates: proof gate — proofs/test_system_rackmount.py, runnable bare (python3) and under the tester. Durability gate: not done until proven AND committed.
falsifier: RED, by physics, on any of: (1) the device serves a callback it did not advertise, or accepts a subscription to an unadvertised name instead of refusing loudly (CP1); (2) a poke fires when the host is UNDER the caller's line, or fails to fire when it is over (the predicate physics must be exact); (3) THE RAW READING CROSSES THE BUS — a poke body (or any envelope) carries the host metric rather than only that the line was crossed (Law 6 broken — the whole point); (4) the device pokes subscribers ITSELF rather than through its shim on the heartbeat pulse (re-absorbing the firing the shim owns), or re-grows a central scheduler / a trigger-kind enum; (5) `SystemRackmountDevice`/`SystemRackmountShim` stop subclassing `BaseDevice`/`BaseShim` / drop CP1-CP6, or `introspect()` stops reporting intention → state → settings in order.
how_it_learns: Yes. (1) The subscriptions + the pokes ARE the learning substrate: the device's state() surfaces who is waiting on which line (never the reading), and every poke is a why-bearing record on the bus — a threshold that never fires and one that fires constantly are both signals in the record, not in silence. (2) Law-6-local-evaluation is itself a learning stance: by keeping the host's data home and exporting only the wake-up, the system learns which host predicates it actually needs to advertise (cpu first, memory/disk as consumers ask) without ever coupling a consumer to the metric — the same start-narrow-and-let-evidence-widen discipline as sudo_relay's box. Its own horizon: the advertised menu (cpu today) and the level-triggered semantics are best-guesses-with-an-expiry — they firm up as real consumers (an ops device, the harvester, a memory-pressure watcher) subscribe.
traces_to: founding intention (get Akien out of running the system — a device gets a host trigger without a human wiring it)
Law 6 (the trigger is evaluated where its data is owned — the host reading never leaves the device; only the poke crosses the bus)
Law 4 (the poke fires by measured condition, not caller goodwill)
Law 5 (intent + code + proofs share this address)
the converged architecture (CairnCommons/intentions-other/I-heartbeat-callbacks-and-bus.md — the system device settles the A/B question → A: owner of host-resource predicates, not a scheduler)
cairn/base/callback.py + cairn/base/shim.py + cairn/ground_loop/loop.py (the universal mechanism this device serves through)
cairn/bus/bus.py (the poke is a bus message)
Akien's correction 2026-07-18 (the rack is the chassis; the system rackmount is the system device, not a scheduler)
owner: CC owns the system_rackmount code + its proofs and gates writes to them. The system device OWNS the host-resource data it serves predicates on; that data is never exported, only pokes are — the owner-gate for host metrics, made physics.
proof: proofs/test_system_rackmount.py — it advertises the cpu_threshold menu and refuses an unadvertised name; end to end through the heartbeat, 'alert me at 80% CPU' pokes the subscriber's feed when over the line and no one when under, with the raw reading (95) crossing the bus nowhere (Law 6); the scheduler goof is gone (no service/scheduler API, no enum); device-hood + shim-hood + Form v0 #2 order. Composes the heartbeat + the real bus (db_domain) + the shim + the Callback. Green bare (python3) and under the tester. FLAKE FOUND + FIXED 2026-07-22 (a learning specimen, Law 8): the Law-6 leak-check scanned the WHOLE envelope for the reading '95', including the bus-assigned random uuid `id` — which contains the hex substring '95' ~12% of the time, so the proof went red ~2/8 by pure chance (a verdict decided by a coin toss, exactly what Law 8 forbids). Measured, not guessed: reproduced 2/8 under concurrency, the failing envelope's body was clean and the '95' sat in the uuid; the check now scopes to device-authored fields (excludes the transport-assigned id/date). Re-proven 0 red over 96 concurrent runs. The 'silent red' was a harness artifact (the earlier loop did not surface the subprocess stderr); under the tester the AssertionError was always captured.
provenance: Born 2026-07-18. First built (mislabeled) as `cairn/rack/` — a 'rack' that was actually a trigger-firing scheduler; then re-homed as a SchedulerService under a service-abstraction framing (cairn 5cb593a). The design conversation with Akien (evening) corrected the model twice over: the rack is the chassis (bus + shims), and the system rackmount is not a scheduler at all but the SYSTEM DEVICE — the owner of host-resource predicates, serving threshold callbacks through the universal heartbeat + shim + bus. Reworked to that shape (built last in the heartbeat+callback+bus rework, composing every piece before it); the SchedulerService + trigger-kind enum deleted. A specimen of learning-its-gates: the identity was corrected by the owner before it hardened, and the code caught up to the corrected intention. Next: the spine resumes (MAP.md:892) — inference domain → web server.
